speaker: interviewer
text: This is March 24, 1995. We're interviewing Rose Leibowitz. And this is tape 4. Rose, you were talking about the liberation. When were you liberated, and who you  were liberated by?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was liberated July 17, 1944, from the Russian Army. And we were-- right away, as we were liberated, went back the 5 kilometers or so to Miedzyrzec, where I was born. Over there, we met some more people. There was about 8, 8 or 12 people hiding in Miedzyrzec in one-- above one of the restaurants. They, they spent all five year there in hiding. And then there was some more people coming in from different parts, from the woods. And some of them were hidden by Polish people. There was one family that was hidden in the town by Polish people. And she came-- and she came in. That's a lady that escaped from Treblinka with her son. She was hidden by a Polish family. Saidi, with her son, Borris, and her niece, Chaviva, which resides now in Israel, and her brother-in-law, and all the other-- all the other people that were liberated. There was-- there must have been, like, about close to a hundred people that accumulated in Miedzyrzec after the liberation, within a week or two weeks. And then the people started to come because the, the-- when the-- as the Russians were liberating, a lot of people-- I, I don't say too many, but some of the people came back to Miedzyrzec. And there was one bad accident. One of the men that came back from Auschwitz and everything-- and the guys were cleaning a gun. And the gun discharged and killed that man. And he was liberated from Auschwitz, lived through the war to just die in Miedzyrzec. And then there were the other people. We just didn't have a means of where to work and where to get our next piece of bread. But some of the people used to be nice. And they used to give us scraps. But then we had a man from Warsaw. And he came in, and he started to bring us everything that we needed. He brought us beds to sleep on, pots and pans where to cook, and all-- and all the food that we could eat. Just-- he was the-- he was the main supplier for us. He was very, very nice to all-- to all of us. Didn't ask for money, didn't ask for anything. He just did it out of the goodness of his heart. His name was Katz. And we were-- as we were liberated and the people were starting to come, some of the Jewish people started to ask for their things to be returned. And-- but that was-- must have been a very bad [NON-ENGLISH] because anybody that ask for things to give them back got killed. But the people didn't ask for nothing in return, they just left them alone. And they didn't touch them. There was a shooting or a killing now and then. And then, as the, the winter came and we didn't have nothing what to wear, and things started to pile up, people were thinking of leaving. But there was no place where to go, and no place where to turn. The Russian Army helped, but very little. It's not the American Army that gave people everything that they needed. They didn't ask if you have food. They didn't ask-- they liberated you. And they were taking-- saving your life. But that was as far as it went. I guess maybe the Polish-- the magistrate, that's-- didn't have enough money to supply us with things, because they didn't ask about it, either. We used to get a card. And you are allowed so much bread and so much of, of another little thing. But that was all. Some of the people that I knew, the Polish people, when they used to come into town, they used to bring me some food. And after a while, my husband got a job, and he started to work. So did some other people. And they made a little money by doing odd jobs. And that's how we supported ourselves. After the winter, when the spring came, it got to be a little easier because we heard that the war was at an end. The, the Germans were just losing everywhere. And we were very hopeful that the war was going to be at an end pretty soon. And we were all thinking of going back-- at that time, Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Histadrut and the-- all the Jewish organizations started to make plans. And at that time, they used to make groups, talk-- what we're going to do in case of this? And what are we going to do in case of that? what-- when things are going to happen? And as-- when the war came to an end, my brother came to our town. He, he-- What do you call it? When they evacuated Auschwitz, he just plain disappeared, he and another two boys. And they came back to our town.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said he's going to Israel. No matter what, he's going to Palestine. And he took off with the boys. And they went away. On the way to Romania, on the way to Italy-- that's where he spent a lot of his time because he just couldn't go to Palestine. Then he wind up in Cyprus. Then from Cyprus, he wind up in Israel. I-- myself and my husband, we were waiting. And I guess it was just meant to be because we said we're going to-- I got sick. I had-- and, and I was-- I couldn't travel with my brother because I was sick. And he said, we'll meet up here in a year. And I said, fine. In the meantime, my cousin came. And when she came-- and we decided we're sitting in Miedzyrzec long enough. That's it. We have to just leave because it's no good to stay here. One of the days I told the-- my neighbors that I'm going to go to the country for the weekend to visit the people that were hiding us. And we're going to spend the weekend with them. We left everything what-- that we owned at that time already, and we went. We left Miedzyrzec. The people later told us that the-- some of the people, especially one Ukraine woman, and she was married to a Jewish man-- said she would kill me herself if she'd known that I was going to run away. We went. We, we knew exactly when the train was passing Miedzyrzec. And we went to the train. And we took the train, and we went to Warsaw. We were about a week in Warsaw. We contacted the-- contacted the Jewish agency. And they gave us-- about 20, 20 to 25 people. We spent there about a week. They put us on a bus to Katowice. When we came to Katowice, to-- the Histadrut and everything, they were all working. Everything was ready for us. When we came there, we spend two, three days. And they send us off to Cracow. We spent in Cracow a few days. And we had places where to stay, food to eat, and everything. And then they sent us to Prague. Naturally, on the border from Poland to Czechoslovakia, we had problems. They wouldn't let us cross the border. But we had-- we were told that we were leaving Poland, that we were not Polish citizens. We didn't have any documents with us. We were just plain displaced persons that are leaving Poland. And we're going to Greece.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they let us pass. And we went to Prague. We came to Prague. The people from the Histadrut took us in. And we spent a few days, quite a few days, with them. I guess we spent-- must have spent a week with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they send us to Bratislava. In Bratislava we spend quite a few days, and we got everything ready. And then they gave my husband a lot of watches. I didn't know that time what they gave him watches for. And we came to Vienna. When we came to Vienna, all the bridges were down. Everything was broken up. And we still-- we left Poland-- was the end of July. And this was already August, the end of August. And we were still-- we couldn't shake the Russians. The Russians were always with us, and they were trying to speak Russian to us. And being that I understood Russian and I could speak, at that time, Russian better than I could now, I-- we were afraid to open our mouths and say something. Because they would realize that we were just escaping, and they wouldn't let us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the Danube. They took us to the Danube. And the, the-- my husband and there was another man, they paid off the Russians in watches to take us across the Danube in kayaks. They took us across the Danube in kayaks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally we came into American zone. When we came to the American zone, we were met again by the Histadrut people, or whatever they were called at that time. And the, the-- they have places for us where to be and where to stay. And then we got registered with the Red Cross, and everything. And we stayed there through the Jewish holidays. It was Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur. And then after Sukkos, they took us to Wels. Wels was a small town not far from Vienna. It was Linz and Wels. And that's where they dispersed the refugees that they-- came in. And we were sent to Wels. We were-- our place were the barracks where the German soldiers used to occupy. There was a lot of rain. And they gave us boots, soldiers' boots. And each time you step in, the boot came off because it was too large on you. So you'd lose the boot. And I was there a month in, in Wels. And I was pregnant at that time already. And my-- and my-- what do you call-- the Red Cross came. And the Red Cross people decided that that's no place for me to be, that they have to send me to a different place. They decided they're going to send me to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took me, my husband, and my cousin. I didn't want to leave without her. They didn't know it was my cousin. They thought it was my sister. I said, I'm not separating from her. If she's not going, I'm not going. So the three of us were sent to Germany. And in Munich, somebody was waiting for us. And they took us to St. Ottilien. There was-- it was already September, towards, I'd say, the first week-- or more than the first week in September.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to St. Ottilien. The only problem with St. Ottilien was that you couldn't stay with your husband or nobody. The men-- there were-- they had big rooms. So they had rooms for men, rooms for ladies, rooms for children. And everybody was separated. I was there until I had to have my daughter. And she was born October 30. So they took me to Landsberg because there were-- excuse me-- there were no facilities for-- maternity facilities in St. Ottilien. So they took me to Landsberg. That's where my daughter was born. And when she was two weeks old, they brought me back to St. Ottilien. And I stayed there again, my husband in one room, me in another room, and my daughter in another room with small children. After four weeks-- four months, I said, I can't live like that. It's-- I'm going nuts. Every time I have to run to see how my child is doing and things. And I started to cry and to beg. So they gave me a little room in Geltendorf. And I was there. That was like about a kilometer-- may have been 2 kilometers from St. Ottilien. And I was there-- we were there for, for about two years or so. I came there in 1945. And I think in 1947, they took us from St. Ottilien. Because that was a monastery before the war, so they gave it back to the-- to the monastery. And they took us to Bad Worishofen. In Bad Worishofen, I spent another-- close to two years, a, a, a-- more than a year and a half. That's when President Truman said that it's good for the people to come to the United States. And we applied because we couldn't go to Israel. As many times as I tried-- when we left Poland, we were actually on the way to Israel. But when we came to Austria and they find out I was pregnant, they wouldn't let me go farther. So I wind up in Germany. My brother wind up in Italy. And I was in Germany until March, 1949, when I was-- got my visa and came to the United States. And, you know, I think I, I, I mentioned the towns that I have been through. And my-- we came-- originally, we came to, to New York. But my husband couldn't find work in New York. So we decided, well, since he had his mother's sisters living in Chicago, we'd go to Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we moved to Chicago. My son was born in Chicago in 1950. And we've been living in Chicago and in the suburbs of Chicago ever since. OK. Rose, have you had-- ever had any different occupations, vocations? Or have you been a housewife this whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been all my life a housewife. My husband used to say, you worked enough for the Germans. For me, you don't have to work. I'm making a living, and we'll live for whatever I'm making. My mother didn't work, and I don't want you to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little about your husband, where you met him and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my husband--

speaker: interviewer
text: --his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in 1939 when the-- when they brought the first refugees to our town. And I-- and when-- the family that lived with me, I went to visit a, a sick friends with her. That's when I first met my husband. And I knew him quite a few years before we, we got serious. And in 1943, we got married. March 1943, we got married. And I thank first God and then my husband I am still alive. Because he used to take me out from so many difficult places, from so many difficult things. Everything he did, he wouldn't let go. He wouldn't let go. I got to go there, and I got to go there. And then this week, I got to hide here because they're going to look for women. And this week I got to do this because they're going to grab them. And, and now that I need him, he's gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us his name and your children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name, my husband's name is Harry [NON-ENGLISH] Leibowitz, and he was born in Poland. We had a daughter which was born in Germany, Paula, her maiden name Leibowitz. Her married name is Paula Friedman. Her husband's name is [? Neil. ?] And they have two lovely children, Sheryl and Steven. And my son, Phil, was born in Chicago. And he's married to a beautiful girl, [? Julie. ?] And they have two lovely children, Mark and Susan. And I'm very lucky to be in the States and to live through all those things and have a nice family because I-- my children have married very nice people. And their families are very nice. And I'm lucky to have a bunch of very nice friends, mostly refugees, but a lot of Americans, too-- and very close, very close friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, so, Rose did your husband, Harry, do all these years when you came to live in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband came-- since he was-- when he was home-- when he was in Poland, they used to be in lumber business. They used to sell lumber. They used to go cut trees in differents woods. They used to buy up things and cut 'em. So he was familiar with lumber.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when we came to the States, one of our friends that we knew said, why don't you become a carpenter? You know what a two-by-four is. You know what a piece of, of wood is. Why don't you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And since he couldn't get any other decent job to make a living, he went and said he's going to try it. And he loved it. He went to school. He took night courses. And my husband, not only was he-- he wasn't a carpenter for a long time. He was a foreman all the years. And he knew how to read a plan. If he would go to school a little bit more, he would be an architect. Because when he took the architect's plan in his hands, he'd put up the most beautiful buildings. He was very good in his profession. And they loved him. He stayed with one company for 24 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of the town you settled in when you first came to live in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I mean, first we were in New York. We were in New York. In-- we stayed with my husband's family one month, with my husband's uncle. Then they rented us a place. And I-- we weren't long in New York. I-- my husband was just there about three months. And I was there another six weeks without him. Then we came to Chicago. And they rented-- we rented a place, 1136 South Homan, if you're familiar. Oh, you're not from Chicago, Illinois. And then we moved to Chicago, to the city. And we lived there 10 years. Then we bought our home in Skokie. We lived in Skokie for 21 years. Now, since my husband started to have heart troubles, and we had to come to the-- to Florida for his health and we-- in the wintertime. We sold our home. And we live in Niles. Niles, Illinois-- that's also a suburb of Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your husband passed away when?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband passed away this last August 11.

speaker: interviewer
text: August 11.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother Jacob?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother Jacob lives in Israel. All-- he went from Italy to Cyprus. From Cyprus, he went to Israel. And that's where he's been ever since. He came to visit me once in the United States. I was a few times in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. And your brother Jacob has done some research on your hometown and--

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother always likes to read and to write, read and write. He's always been like that. We were always brought up in books. I loved to read books myself. Now I can't do it so much-- my eyes are--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --bothering me. But I used to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did your brother return to Miedzyrzec to research, to publish the history?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no. It's just from, from memory--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --just from memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in Miedzyrzec that time when he-- when he came back from Miedzyrzec. But he was a short time in Miedzyrzec, maybe a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he went right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he left for Lublin. And then he went to Romania. And then he went to Italy. And then he was caught on the way to Israel-- they sent him to Cyprus. And then he came to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in all the wars in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. So he-- was he in the underground--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --when he first came?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the-- He was in the underground. He was laying three days in Latrun. They didn't know he was going to be alive. I didn't hear nothing from him for three months. I was going nuts. He was in all the wars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a-- he was a-- he's a good Israeli soldier--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --man. Israel needs a lot of people like him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So have you been--

speaker: interviewee
text: Believe me. And his children, too, they wouldn't think of coming here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been back to Miedzyrzec at all or visited?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nope. When I left at that time, that was it. And I don't have a feeling to go back because I don't have what to go back to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's not even a grave to go back to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'd just-- I'd rather go-- I'd rather go to Israel. When I go to Israel, everybody says, are you going to go to London? Are you going to go here? Are you going to go there? I'm not going nowhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's all I want to do is spend the time with my brother there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have time for anything else. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you told you children much about your experiences, as you're telling us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my children know. I think my children know everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandchildren know, too. As a matter of fact, when they, they have to have some essays for school or something, Grandma, tell me this and this. Grandma, tell me this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We always talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband never liked to talk about it. Because, I guess, he was very hurt because he really didn't have nobody left, not even-- not even one first cousin. His family all disappeared from this earth. He had family on his mother's side in the United States. But on his father's side, he doesn't-- absolutely nobody left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody left on his father's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to tell us now, Rose, in your own words, why you felt to give us your testimony and tell us what happened with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I always thought I was going to do it. My children, especially my [? Julie, ?] my daughter-in-law, she was after me. She says, Ma, you've got to tell it. Ma, you have to tell it. You have to do it. We want to know. She just wants to make a tree from all the families, from this and from that. She's always asking and writing things. She always-- she wants to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all. My daughter wants to know. She-- they-- my kids are very Jewish-minded, thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are-- that's the way they were brought up. They were brought up in a temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We always belonged to a temple. My kids belong to a temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess we are-- that's the way we are. That's the way we were brought up in Miedzyrzec.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Always help people, do for people, and, and be proud of who you are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we certainly appreciate your giving us your testimony. I'm sure many people will benefit from hearing this. And thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I'm glad I'm still alive to do it. It's not too many alive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't think I was going to be able to do it. This is my mother. Her name is Perl Goldstein. She died a year before the war. And I lost her when I was real young. I was-- I was not quite 14 years old yet. And I always miss her. I don't know where I came about this picture. I think my brother had it somewhere, and he gave it to me. She was very young in this picture. She looks like she was in her early 20s. This is Sheindele Leibowitz. It's my husband's mother. I met her in 1939 when she first came to Miedzyrzec. She was born in Nasielsk, Poland. She was a beautiful woman and a marvelous person. She took care not only on her children, but her husband's whole family when they brought them to Poland, to Miedzyrzec during the war years. She was a go-getter. And I always loved-- and I think she loved me, too. [CRYING] I know for a short time. This is my husband's father, Faivl Leibowitz. Faivl Leibowitz was born in Pultusk, Poland. He was a very beautiful person, tall man, very good to his family. I knew him for about four years. He was always nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me, Rose Leibowitz. I guess I like to go by the name of Ruchel, too. My husband used to call me Ruchla. And I-- at, at this age, I met my husband. I was 16 years old then. And that picture was made in Miedzyrzec, Poland, just about half a year before the war broke out. This is a river near Miedzyrzec. And this is one of the jobs I was working from, regulating the river and digging dirt with a lot of other people. That's in Miedzyrzec Podlaski. That was in 1943. This is my brother Jacob-- lives in Israel. And this, I guess, is a picture that he took with a friend of his, working. He's-- got together somethings. And he wrote about Miedzyrzec, our town, our hometown in Poland. And he loves to write about things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I guess this is one of the things that he tried to put together. I don't know where he got the material from, but I know a lot is from his remembrance. And this was, I think, taken in-- made in '85, 1985. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Harry. Everybody called him Herchel And I think he was a very lovable man because everybody just liked to be around him and people just raving about him. I met him in 1939. We knew each other for quite a few years-- for four years before we got married during the war. And I just lost him in August of '94. And I miss him a lot. [CRYING] I wish he was here. [SIGHS] He was 74 in this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: He died here, or he died in Illinois?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Illinois, in the hospital with doctors around. Took him to the hospital Sunday morning, 1:30. He didn't want to be in the hospital. We transferred him to the hospital where he knew the doctors. And the doctor said, everything is all right. And I saw him there. I went-- I was with my daughter there. We were there 4:30 in the morning. They were going to operate on him. They got everything ready. And, and, and that's it. [SIGHS]
